candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture distinguish legitimacy as an equilibrium of iterated interaction from legitimacy grounded in normative justification, coercion, or identity attachment? The open question is whether repeated reciprocal play is a sufficient explanation of institutional legitimacy, or only one mechanism among several that can substitute for or mask each other under rapid change.

What observable structural indicators actually measure whether an institution has preserved 'the game structure' under turbulence? This raises the unresolved problem of operationalization: how to detect time-horizon collapse, loss of legibility, reputation fragmentation, and failed credible adaptation in ways that allow comparison across institutions and prediction of legitimacy breakdown before it becomes visible.

Under what conditions can institutions intentionally redesign interfaces, feedback loops, and memory mechanisms to preserve reciprocity without becoming manipulatively performative or overly rigid? The tension is that the same devices meant to sustain repeated play may also simulate accountability, entrench power, or prevent necessary rule change, so the conjecture needs a theory of when legitimacy-preserving adaptation becomes legitimacy-eroding theater.
